# American Arbitration Association
## Construction Industry Arbitration Rules and Mediation Procedures
### Practitioner Manual — Selected Sections with Commentary

*Excerpt from the AAA-ICDR Practice Manual, Section 7 ("Construction Industry Rules — Annotated"), 2024 Edition. Reproduced under license. Not legal advice. Refer to the current published Rules at adr.org for the controlling text.*

---

## Introduction to Section 7

---

## Introduction to Section 7

The American Arbitration Association first promulgated dedicated **Construction Industry Arbitration Rules** in 1966, in response to growing demand from the construction sector — and particularly from the American Institute of Architects, the Associated General Contractors of America, and the American Subcontractors Association — for a procedural framework calibrated to the realities of construction disputes. The Rules have been amended on average every five to seven years since, with the most recent amendments effective **March 1, 2024**.

This Section reproduces selected key Rules with brief practitioner commentary, but does not substitute for direct consultation of the published Rules. Practitioners are reminded that the AAA's published Rules are amended periodically and that the version in force at the time of the demand controls.

---

## R-1. Agreement of Parties; Standard, Procedures for Large, Complex Construction Disputes

### Rule Text (Summary)

---

## R-1. Agreement of Parties; Standard, Procedures for Large, Complex Construction Disputes

### Rule Text (Summary)

The Construction Industry Arbitration Rules apply whenever the parties have agreed to arbitration under the AAA's Construction Industry Rules. Where the disputed amount exceeds **$500,000** exclusive of interest, attorneys' fees, and costs (or where the parties stipulate), the **Procedures for Large, Complex Construction Disputes** ("LCC Procedures") apply in addition to the regular Rules.

### Practitioner Commentary

The LCC Procedures are not optional in qualifying matters. They impose additional case-management requirements, including:

---

## R-2. AAA and Delegation of Duties

The AAA, in administering an arbitration, shall be responsible for the proper application of the Rules and the proper appointment of the panel. The AAA's Construction Division operates from offices in **New York, Atlanta, Dallas, and Los Angeles**, and is staffed by case managers with subject-matter training in construction practice.

### Footnote 7.2: AAA Case Manager Roles

A AAA case manager is not an arbitrator and does not decide substantive issues. The case manager's role is logistical: scheduling, fee collection, panel rotation, and communication facilitation. Practitioners should not direct substantive correspondence to the case manager.

---

## R-3. National Roster of Arbitrators

### Rule Text (Summary)

---

## R-3. National Roster of Arbitrators

### Rule Text (Summary)

The AAA maintains a **National Roster** of approximately 4,800 arbitrators, of whom approximately 1,200 are credentialed on the **Construction Panel**. A subset of approximately 240 arbitrators is further credentialed on the **Large, Complex Case Panel**.

Construction Panel credentialing requires (i) a minimum of fifteen years of construction-related professional experience; (ii) demonstrable arbitration experience or completion of the AAA's Construction Arbitrator Training program; and (iii) successful completion of a periodic re-credentialing review.

### Practitioner Commentary

### Practitioner Commentary

The practical effect of the credentialing structure is that party-list selection in construction matters draws from a smaller and more specialized pool than in commercial matters generally. Practitioners should not assume that an arbitrator's listed availability on the National Roster signals construction credentialing.

---

## R-7. Initiation Under an Arbitration Provision in a Contract

### Filing Demand

The claimant initiates arbitration by filing a written **Demand for Arbitration** with the AAA, accompanied by (i) the applicable filing fee per the fee schedule in effect on the filing date; (ii) a copy of the arbitration agreement; and (iii) a description of the dispute, the relief sought, and the names of the parties.

### Service

### Footnote 7.10: Locale Disputes

Where the parties have not agreed on a locale, the AAA will determine the locale considering: (i) the location of the project; (ii) the location of the parties and counsel; (iii) the location of relevant witnesses; (iv) the location of relevant documents; and (v) any locale provisions in the underlying contract. Locale disputes are heard by the AAA's administrator; the determination is administrative and not subject to interlocutory review.

---

## R-13. Direct Appointment by a Party

---

## R-13. Direct Appointment by a Party

In a three-arbitrator construction case, the parties may have agreed in their contract to a "party-appointed" structure, in which each party directly appoints one arbitrator and the two party-appointees select the chair. The AAA defaults, however, to a **fully neutral, AAA-appointed** structure under which all arbitrators are selected from the National Roster by a party-strike process.

### Practitioner Note

### Practitioner Note

The 2024 amendments emphasized the presumption of fully-neutral panels even in cases governed by older "party-appointed" contractual language, by providing that party-appointed arbitrators are subject to the same disclosure and impartiality obligations as the chair. The practitioner should not assume the kind of "advocate-arbitrator" structure familiar from older construction practice.

---

## R-15. Number of Arbitrators

The default panel size for matters under **$1,000,000** is one arbitrator; the default for matters between $1M and $10M is one or three by AAA determination; the default for matters over $10M is three.

These defaults are subject to party agreement.

---

## R-19. Disclosure

These defaults are subject to party agreement.

---

## R-19. Disclosure

Each appointed arbitrator must complete a **Notice of Appointment and Disclosure Statement** within **seven (7) days** of notice of appointment. The disclosure must include:

1. Any financial, business, professional, family, or social relationships with the parties or counsel within the prior two years;
2. Any matter in which the arbitrator has previously served as a neutral involving any of the parties or counsel;
3. Any other circumstance reasonably likely to create a presumption of bias or affect the arbitrator's impartiality.

The duty of disclosure is **continuing** and extends through the rendering of the final award.

### Footnote 7.19: Standards Beyond the AAA Rules

The duty of disclosure is **continuing** and extends through the rendering of the final award.

### Footnote 7.19: Standards Beyond the AAA Rules

The disclosure standards under California's **Code of Civil Procedure §§ 1281.9 and 1281.85**, and the **Ethics Standards for Neutral Arbitrators in Contractual Arbitration** promulgated by the California Judicial Council, may impose disclosure obligations beyond those of the AAA Rules. Arbitrators serving in California-seated matters must comply with the higher standard.

---

## R-23. Preliminary Management Hearing

Within **fifteen (15) days** of the appointment of the panel (LCC track) or **twenty-one (21) days** (regular track), the panel will convene a preliminary management hearing. The PMH agenda typically includes:

### Practitioner Commentary

The PMH is the single most important procedural event in a construction arbitration. Practitioners who treat it as administrative invite avoidable cost and delay. Pre-PMH meet-and-confer between counsel is strongly recommended, and the proposed scheduling order should be filed in joint form, with disputed items clearly marked.

---

## R-24. Discovery; Information Exchange

The Construction Rules contemplate a **proportional** approach to discovery. The default scope is the exchange of documents on which a party relies for its claims or defenses, plus targeted document requests. Depositions are not assumed and are typically limited (e.g., one to three per side) absent good cause shown.

### Footnote 7.24: E-Discovery

### Footnote 7.24: E-Discovery

Where electronic information is significant — which in construction practice has become the rule rather than the exception, given email-heavy project files and BIM (Building Information Modeling) data — the panel will typically issue an **ESI protocol** at the PMH, drawing on the Sedona Conference's *Commentary on Proportionality in Electronic Discovery*.

---

## R-31. Evidence

The arbitrator(s) shall determine the admissibility, relevance, and materiality of evidence. The strict rules of evidence are **not applicable**. The arbitrator may receive hearsay, opinion evidence, and evidence in summary form, weighing each according to its reliability.

### Practitioner Commentary

### Practitioner Commentary

Notwithstanding the relaxed evidentiary standards, panel-experienced counsel will continue to lay foundation for expert testimony and to object to demonstrably unreliable evidence. The relaxed standards govern *admissibility*; they do not preclude argument about *weight*.

---

## R-39. Time of Award

The panel shall render the final award **no later than thirty (30) days from the date of the close of the hearing**, or — if briefs are submitted post-hearing — thirty (30) days from the submission of the briefs.

### Footnote 7.39: Extensions

The thirty-day deadline is subject to extension by the AAA upon a showing of good cause. In LCC matters with extensive records, extensions of an additional thirty (30) to sixty (60) days are not uncommon.

---

---

## R-44. Form of Award

A construction award may be (i) a **standard award** (operative provisions only); (ii) a **reasoned award**; or (iii) **findings of fact and conclusions of law**. The default in LCC matters is the reasoned award. The practitioner should clarify form at the PMH to avoid surprise at the back end of the case.

---

## R-49. Administrative Fees

### Filing Fees

The AAA's filing fee for a Construction Industry Demand is calibrated to the amount in controversy, ranging from **$1,750** for matters under $10,000 to **$15,500** for matters between $10,000,001 and $50,000,000. Matters over $50,000,000 are charged on an hourly or flat-fee negotiated basis.

### Case Service Fee

### Case Service Fee

In addition to the filing fee, the AAA charges a **case service fee** payable at the time the case is scheduled for hearing. The case service fee is also calibrated to the amount in controversy.

### Footnote 7.49: Fee Allocation

The default allocation is that the parties share administrative fees equally, unless the contract or applicable law (e.g., consumer-protection statutes) requires otherwise. The panel may, in the final award, reallocate administrative fees as part of the costs of the proceeding.

---

## R-58. Fast Track Procedures (Disputes Under $100,000)

The Fast Track applies, by default, to construction disputes in which no party has asserted a claim or counterclaim exceeding $100,000, exclusive of interest and fees. The Fast Track provides:

The following is a reference scheduling order frequently used by the AAA's Construction Division. It is illustrative only.

> ORDER NO. 1 — PRELIMINARY SCHEDULING ORDER
>
> 1. **Document Exchange.** The parties shall exchange documents on which they intend to rely no later than [DATE].
>
> 2. **Site Visit.** A site visit shall occur on [DATE] at [LOCATION], jointly coordinated by counsel. The arbitrator(s) may attend.
>
> 3. **Expert Reports.** Initial expert reports shall be served by [DATE]. Rebuttal reports by [DATE].
>
> 4. **Depositions.** Each party may take up to [N] depositions, each not to exceed seven (7) hours.
>
> 5. **Dispositive Motions.** Any dispositive motion shall be filed by [DATE].
>
> 6. **Merits Hearing.** The merits hearing shall commence on [DATE] at [LOCATION] and shall continue for [N] business days.
>
> 7. **Post-Hearing Briefs.** Simultaneous opening briefs by [DATE]; reply briefs by [DATE].
>
> 8. **Form of Award.** The panel shall render a [STANDARD / REASONED / FF&CL] award.
>
> 9. **Confidentiality.** All filings and materials produced in this proceeding are confidential and shall not be disclosed to third parties except as required by law.

---

## Section 7.B — Selected Statistics (2023 AAA Construction Caseload)

- **Total construction-caseload filings, 2023:** 1,114 (down 4.8% from 2022)
- **Average claim size (LCC track):** $14.7 million
- **Average claim size (regular track):** $810,000
- **Median time from filing to award (regular track):** 11.2 months
- **Median time from filing to award (LCC track):** 18.4 months
- **Percentage of matters resolved before final award:** 38%
- **Percentage of awards subject to motion to vacate in court:** approximately 7%
- **Percentage of motions to vacate that succeed:** approximately 1.5% of awards (i.e., roughly 20% of motions filed)

---

## Section 7.C — Glossary of Key Terms

---

## Section 7.C — Glossary of Key Terms

- **BIM (Building Information Modeling):** An integrated digital model of a project's physical and functional characteristics, used in design, construction, and operation.
- **CPM Schedule (Critical Path Method):** A scheduling methodology used to identify and manage the sequence of project activities that determines minimum project duration.
- **Differing Site Conditions Clause:** A contractual clause allocating risk between owner and contractor for site conditions that materially differ from those indicated in the contract documents.
- **Liquidated Damages Clause:** A contractual clause specifying a per-day or per-event sum payable as agreed damages for delay, in lieu of actual proof of damages.
- **Mechanic's Lien:** A statutory security interest in real property in favor of contractors, subcontractors, and material suppliers who have not been paid for work or materials.
- **Pay-When-Paid / Pay-If-Paid Clauses:** Contractual provisions that condition a contractor's obligation to pay a subcontractor on the contractor's receipt of payment from the owner. Treatment varies by jurisdiction.
- **Schedule of Values:** A contractor's allocation of the total contract price among the various components of the work, used to support progress payments.

---

## Section 7.E — Case-Management Checklists

The following checklists, drawn from AAA case-manager training materials, summarize the procedural milestones the panel and the parties should track in a typical regular-track and LCC-track matter.

### 7.E.1 — Regular-Track Construction Arbitration

### 7.E.1 — Regular-Track Construction Arbitration

- [ ] Demand filed and served
- [ ] Filing fee paid
- [ ] Respondent's answer received (Day 14)
- [ ] Administrative conference held (Day 14-21)
- [ ] Panel size and locale determined
- [ ] Arbitrator strike lists circulated
- [ ] Strike lists returned (Day 30-37)
- [ ] Panel appointed
- [ ] Notice of Appointment and Disclosure circulated (Day 38)
- [ ] Disclosure responses received from parties (Day 45)
- [ ] Preliminary management hearing scheduled (within 21 days of appointment)
- [ ] Order No. 1 — Preliminary Scheduling Order entered
- [ ] Document exchange deadline (typically 60-120 days)
- [ ] Site visit (if applicable)
- [ ] Expert reports exchanged (initial + rebuttal)
- [ ] Deposition phase complete
- [ ] Dispositive motion phase complete (if applicable)
- [ ] Final pre-hearing conference
- [ ] Pre-hearing briefs filed
- [ ] Merits hearing held
- [ ] Post-hearing briefs filed
- [ ] Hearing closed
- [ ] Award rendered (within 30 days)
- [ ] Award transmitted by AAA

### 7.E.2 — LCC-Track Construction Arbitration

The LCC track shares the regular-track milestones but adds:

### 7.E.2 — LCC-Track Construction Arbitration

The LCC track shares the regular-track milestones but adds:

- [ ] LCC-track designation confirmed at administrative conference
- [ ] Three-arbitrator panel selected
- [ ] Chair designation confirmed (typically by AAA from highest-ranked listed candidate or by party agreement)
- [ ] Preliminary management hearing held within **15 days** of panel appointment (shorter than regular track)
- [ ] ESI protocol entered
- [ ] BIM-data protocol entered (if applicable)
- [ ] Multi-phase scheduling order entered, often including:
  - Liability phase
  - Damages phase
  - Allocation phase (in multi-party matters)
- [ ] Expert phasing protocol (often multiple rounds)
- [ ] Hot-tubbing protocol entered (if used)
- [ ] Mid-case status conference (typically every 60 days)
- [ ] Reasoned award rendered

### 7.E.3 — Fast-Track Construction Arbitration

- [ ] Demand filed and served
- [ ] Filing fee paid
- [ ] Respondent's answer received (Day 14)
- [ ] Telephonic administrative conference held (Day 7-14)
- [ ] Single arbitrator appointed (Day 21-30)
- [ ] Telephonic preliminary management hearing held within 7 days
- [ ] Limited document exchange complete (typically 30-45 days)
- [ ] Merits hearing held (within 60 days of appointment)
- [ ] Award rendered (within 14 days of close of hearing)

---

## Section 7.F — Frequently Asked Questions

### Q: Can a party invoke the Construction Industry Rules where the contract refers only to "AAA arbitration"?

### Q: Can a party invoke the Construction Industry Rules where the contract refers only to "AAA arbitration"?

A: Where the contract refers to AAA arbitration without specifying a rule set, the AAA administrator will determine which rule set best fits the dispute based on the nature of the underlying contract and the subject matter of the dispute. A pure construction dispute under a construction contract will almost always be administered under the Construction Industry Rules. Mixed disputes — for example, a construction contract with a significant ancillary services component — may be administered under the Commercial Rules with construction-specific procedural orders.

### Q: Can a respondent compel arbitration of a claim filed in court?

### Q: Can a respondent compel arbitration of a claim filed in court?

A: Yes, subject to a properly-pleaded motion to compel arbitration in the relevant court under Section 4 of the Federal Arbitration Act or, for California-seated matters, Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.2. The Rules themselves do not address the motion-to-compel procedure; that is a matter of court procedure.

### Q: What is the effect of a contractual waiver of consequential damages?

### Q: What is the effect of a contractual waiver of consequential damages?

A: A contractual waiver of consequential damages, common in construction contracts, is generally enforceable in arbitration under the Construction Rules. The arbitrator will give effect to the contractual allocation of risk. The arbitrator may, however, scrutinize a damages-waiver clause where the asserted damages overlap categories ("delay damages" vs. "consequential damages") or where the clause is alleged to be unconscionable.

### Q: How does the Construction Rules' treatment of dispositive motions differ from the Commercial Rules?

### Q: How does the Construction Rules' treatment of dispositive motions differ from the Commercial Rules?

A: The Construction Rules contemplate dispositive motions but do not require any particular procedural framework. The panel's case-management discretion is broad. Practice has converged, in larger construction matters, on a model that requires (i) advance leave from the panel to file a dispositive motion; (ii) a clear identification of the specific issue or claim the motion seeks to dispose of; and (iii) a showing of why early disposition will materially advance the proceeding. The panel may decline leave where it concludes the issue is better addressed on a full evidentiary record.

### Q: When does an arbitrator's duty to disclose end?

### Q: When does an arbitrator's duty to disclose end?

A: The duty is **continuing**. It extends through the rendering of the final award (and, in some circumstances, through the resolution of any post-award proceedings). A circumstance arising during the proceeding that would have required disclosure had it existed at the time of appointment must be disclosed at the time it arises.

### Q: What is the difference between a "reasoned" award and "findings of fact and conclusions of law"?

### Q: What is the difference between a "reasoned" award and "findings of fact and conclusions of law"?

A: A **reasoned award** sets out, in narrative form, the basis for the panel's decision. It need not include formal findings of fact in numbered paragraphs or conclusions of law in the manner of a federal court opinion. **Findings of fact and conclusions of law** ("FF&CL") are more formal and resemble the output of a court trial. Parties should clarify the desired form at the preliminary management hearing.

### Q: Are arbitration awards under the Construction Rules confidential?

### Q: Are arbitration awards under the Construction Rules confidential?

A: The Rules contain a confidentiality provision binding the AAA and the arbitrators. The Rules do not, of their own force, bind the parties to maintain the confidentiality of the proceedings or the award. Where confidentiality is important, the parties typically enter a separate confidentiality order at the preliminary management hearing.

---

## Section 7.G — Selected Case Law on Construction Arbitration

The following cases are useful starting points for the practitioner researching common issues in construction arbitration. (This is illustrative only and is not a comprehensive citation list.)

- **Stolt-Nielsen S.A. v. AnimalFeeds Int'l Corp.**, 559 U.S. 662 (2010) — class arbitration availability.
- **Lamps Plus, Inc. v. Varela**, 587 U.S. ___ (2019) — ambiguity in arbitration agreements and the class question.
- **Henry Schein, Inc. v. Archer & White Sales, Inc.**, 586 U.S. ___ (2019) — delegation clauses and the "wholly groundless" exception.
- **Coinbase, Inc. v. Bielski**, 599 U.S. ___ (2023) — automatic stay of district-court proceedings on interlocutory appeal of order denying motion to compel arbitration.
- **Smith v. Spizzirri**, 601 U.S. ___ (2024) — mandatory stay (rather than dismissal) after order compelling arbitration.
- *(California-specific)* **Iskanian v. CLS Transportation Los Angeles, LLC**, 59 Cal. 4th 348 (2014) — PAGA carve-out, partially abrogated by Viking River Cruises.
- *(California-specific)* **Viking River Cruises v. Moriana**, 596 U.S. ___ (2022) — PAGA arbitration.
- *(California-specific)* **Adolph v. Uber Technologies, Inc.**, 14 Cal. 5th 1104 (2023) — standing after Viking River.
- *(California-specific)* **Honeycutt v. Vertix Solutions**, 102 Cal. App. 5th 412 (2024) *(hypothetical citation for illustration)* — disclosure obligations.

The construction practitioner should additionally be familiar with the principal cases on:

- **Differing site conditions** doctrine
- **Spearin doctrine** (owner-supplied specifications)
- **No-damages-for-delay** clauses and exceptions
- **Mechanics' lien** waivers under the various state lien statutes
- **Pay-when-paid / pay-if-paid** enforceability variations
- **Mutual mistake** in construction contract formation
- **Liquidated damages** vs. penalty doctrines

## Section 7.I — Practitioner Tips

Drawn from interviews with experienced construction arbitrators and counsel conducted for the 2024 edition of this manual:

1. **"Pleading the dispute" matters.** A clear, well-pleaded Demand reduces administrative back-and-forth and frames the dispute for the panel from Day 1.

2. **The PMH is not pro forma.** Treat it as the single most important procedural event in the case.

2. **The PMH is not pro forma.** Treat it as the single most important procedural event in the case.

3. **Joint scheduling orders save time.** Counsel who work out a joint proposal in advance — with disputed items clearly marked — save the panel meaningful time and earn credibility.

4. **Expert phasing reduces cost.** Phased exchange of expert reports (initial, rebuttal, sur-rebuttal where allowed) generally reduces total expert spend versus a single round.

5. **Schedule hot-tubbing.** In matters with technical disputes, joint expert testimony ("hot-tubbing") often produces clearer panel understanding than sequential direct-and-cross.

6. **Do not over-discover.** Construction matters generate vast paper. Counsel who discipline their requests focus the panel on what actually matters.

6. **Do not over-discover.** Construction matters generate vast paper. Counsel who discipline their requests focus the panel on what actually matters.

7. **Treat the panel as a panel.** In three-arbitrator matters, counsel who treat all three panelists as decision-makers (rather than addressing only the chair) tend to be more successful.

8. **Get the form of award right at the PMH.** Surprise about award form at the back end of the case is avoidable.

9. **Plan for post-award.** Even confirmed awards may need additional procedural steps. Counsel who anticipate the post-award phase reduce friction.

10. **Read the contract.** It is surprising how often counsel arrive at the PMH without a clear command of the controlling contractual provisions.

---

# The Class That Built a Generation: Berkeley Law's 1980s Boalt Hall Alumni in Public Life

*Berkeley Law Transcript — Alumni Magazine, Winter 2024 / Vol. 47, No. 4*
By Jessamyn Park, Class of 2002

---

## A Decade in Retrospect

The 1980s at **Boalt Hall School of Law** — as it was still called until the school's 2020 rebranding — were a transformative decade. The school admitted its first class above 50% women in 1984. It launched the **Center for Social Justice** (then the East Bay Community Law Center, in its earliest incubation form) in 1988. It hosted, in 1986, the famously contentious all-day faculty colloquium that produced what became the founding documents of the **Critical Legal Studies** movement on the West Coast.

It also graduated, between 1980 and 1989, roughly **2,950** lawyers, of whom a remarkable number have shaped the legal, civic, political, judicial, academic, and entrepreneurial landscape of California — and of the country — in the four decades since.

This issue's feature profiles **fourteen** of them. They are by no means a comprehensive list. They are, instead, a representative cross-section chosen to capture the range of paths a Boalt JD has opened in the years since the 1980s.

A note on omissions: this feature was reported between June and September 2024. Several alumni who would otherwise have been profiled — including the late **Hon. Ronald T. Maverick '83**, who passed away in March 2024 — could not be included for reasons of timing. The editors apologize for those gaps and intend to address them in future issues.

---

## Profile 1: Hon. Bernadette Singh-Carrillo, JD '81

The Honorable Bernadette Singh-Carrillo currently serves as **Presiding Justice of the California Court of Appeal, Fourth Appellate District, Division Two**, based in Riverside. She was appointed to the appellate bench by Governor Davis in 2001 and elevated to Presiding Justice by Governor Newsom in 2020.

Before her judicial appointment, Justice Singh-Carrillo spent fifteen years in private practice at the Los Angeles firm of **Munger, Tolles & Olson**, where she became the firm's third woman partner in 1992. She specialized in complex commercial litigation and First Amendment defense work, representing media clients including the *Los Angeles Times* and the McClatchy newspaper group.

Justice Singh-Carrillo grew up in the Central Valley town of **Visalia**, the eldest of four children of Punjabi-Mexican-American parents who ran a citrus-packing operation. She attended **UCLA** for her undergraduate degree (English, 1978) before coming to Berkeley.

She is a regular speaker at Berkeley Law's judicial-clerkship workshops and a longtime supporter of the school's **First-Generation Professional Students** affinity group.

## Profile 2: Caleb O. Foxworth, JD '82

**Caleb Foxworth** is the co-founder and managing partner of **Foxworth Yi Aldermire LLP**, a San Francisco-based boutique tax-controversy firm with offices in San Francisco, San Diego, and Washington, D.C. The firm represents high-net-worth individuals and family offices in federal and state tax disputes.

Foxworth began his career at the **Internal Revenue Service's Office of Chief Counsel** in Washington, where he worked for seven years before returning to California to join the predecessor of his current firm in 1989. He became the firm's named partner in 2004 after the retirement of one of its original founders.

Foxworth is a graduate of **Morehouse College** (1979), a former chair of the **State Bar of California's Taxation Section**, and a sitting member of the **American Law Institute**. He is also a longtime supporter of the **Berkeley Law Tax Law Clinic**, which his firm endowed in 2018.

He and his husband, the architect **Tomas Aldermire**, live in San Francisco's Cole Valley neighborhood.

## Profile 3: Aisha Nakimuli-Brown, JD '82

He and his husband, the architect **Tomas Aldermire**, live in San Francisco's Cole Valley neighborhood.

## Profile 3: Aisha Nakimuli-Brown, JD '82

After a career trajectory that included six years at **Cravath, Swaine & Moore** in New York and twelve years at the **U.S. Department of Justice's Antitrust Division**, **Aisha Nakimuli-Brown** is currently the **General Counsel of Patagonia, Inc.**, a position she has held since 2018.

Nakimuli-Brown was the first Black woman in Patagonia's senior leadership team and remains one of a small number of Black general counsel of large privately held U.S. companies. She is widely credited with leading Patagonia's ownership-restructuring transaction in 2022, under which the founding Chouinard family transferred ownership of the company to a purpose trust dedicated to environmental causes.

She has published widely on corporate purpose, environmental governance, and antitrust enforcement, including a recent *Stanford Law Review* article on the role of B Corporation status in merger analysis.

She holds an undergraduate degree from **Spelman College** (1979) and an LL.M. in International Legal Studies from **NYU** (1991).

## Profile 4: Vincent A. Marquardt-Spinelli, JD '83

## Profile 4: Vincent A. Marquardt-Spinelli, JD '83

**Vincent Marquardt-Spinelli** has spent the entirety of his post-Boalt career at one institution: the **Federal Public Defender's Office for the Northern District of California**, where he is now the Office's Chief of the Capital Habeas Unit.

In his thirty-one years with the office, Marquardt-Spinelli has represented more than forty death-row inmates at various stages of federal habeas litigation. He successfully argued *Marquardt v. Ayers* before the Ninth Circuit in 2008 (an unrelated party of the same surname), establishing a procedural standard for evidentiary hearings in California capital cases that remains in force.

In 2019 he received the **Bryan Stevenson Award for Capital Defense** from the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers, the highest national award in the field.

Marquardt-Spinelli grew up in **Buffalo, New York**. He came to Berkeley after college at SUNY Binghamton because, he says, "I had never seen mountains and I wanted to."

## Profile 5: Helena Wuwurong-Yuan, JD '83

**Helena Wuwurong-Yuan** is the founder and CEO of **Veridia Health**, a Boston-based health-technology company that develops decision-support software for hospital intensive-care units. The company was founded in 2014 and reached "unicorn" valuation in late 2022.

Before her transition to entrepreneurship, Wuwurong-Yuan practiced healthcare regulatory law for twenty-two years, including ten years at **Ropes & Gray** in Boston and twelve years as Associate General Counsel at **Partners HealthCare** (now Mass General Brigham).

She is a graduate of **Wellesley College** (1980), holds an MBA from **MIT Sloan** (2013), and recently joined the board of trustees of **Boston Children's Hospital**.

She has endowed the **Wuwurong-Yuan Fellowship** at Berkeley Law, which supports a 2L or 3L pursuing summer work in health-law policy.

## Profile 6: Reginald A. Cervantes-Owusu, JD '84

## Profile 6: Reginald A. Cervantes-Owusu, JD '84

After fifteen years of progressively senior roles in the **California Office of Legislative Counsel** in Sacramento, **Reginald Cervantes-Owusu** became, in 2014, the **Legislative Counsel of California** — the chief drafter and counsel to the California Legislature. He retired from that position in May 2024 after a decade of service.

Cervantes-Owusu is widely credited with modernizing the office's drafting and bill-tracking infrastructure, including the 2018 launch of the **legislative-text public API** that now powers much of the press and civil-society coverage of California legislation.

He attended **UC Davis** as an undergraduate (Political Science, 1981), grew up in **East Palo Alto**, and now lives in retirement in **Carmel-by-the-Sea**, where he says he gardens and writes "very bad fiction." He has been invited, but has not yet agreed, to teach a seminar at Berkeley Law on legislative drafting.

## Profile 7: Magdalena Sokolova, JD '84

**Magdalena Sokolova** is the **Chief Judge of the U.S. Court of International Trade**, based in New York. She was nominated to the court by President Obama in 2014 and elevated to Chief Judge by seniority in 2022.

Before her appointment, Sokolova spent twenty-six years in the international trade practice of **Hogan Lovells** (and its predecessor firm Hogan & Hartson), serving for the last eight as the firm's global trade practice chair. She represented sovereigns, importers, and U.S. industry petitioners in antidumping, countervailing duty, and customs matters.

Sokolova was born in **Prague**, then in Czechoslovakia, and emigrated with her family in 1968. She is a graduate of **Smith College** (1981). She has lectured at Berkeley Law twice in the past decade on issues of trade-remedy law.

## Profile 8: Daniel Ngarambe-Patel, JD '85

## Profile 8: Daniel Ngarambe-Patel, JD '85

**Daniel Ngarambe-Patel** is the founding executive director of the **Pacific Coast Immigrant Rights Center**, a Los Angeles-based legal-services and advocacy organization that he founded in 1992.

Under his leadership, the Center has grown from a three-attorney shop operating out of a borrowed storefront to a 110-employee organization with offices in Los Angeles, Fresno, San Diego, and Seattle. The Center is responsible for the legal representation of an estimated 14,000 immigration clients annually.

Ngarambe-Patel was awarded a **MacArthur Foundation Fellowship** in 2006 and the **ABA Medal** in 2019, the highest honor of the American Bar Association.

He clerked, after Boalt, for **Judge Stephen Reinhardt** of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. He keeps a framed letter from Judge Reinhardt on the wall of his Los Angeles office.

## Profile 9: Patricia "Patty" Dornbusch-Kim, JD '85

After clerkships at the Ninth Circuit and the U.S. Supreme Court (for Justice Sandra Day O'Connor), **Patty Dornbusch-Kim** joined the faculty of **Stanford Law School** in 1988 and was tenured in 1994. She held the **Marion Rice Kirkwood Professorship of Law** from 2008 until her appointment to the **Ninth Circuit** in 2014.

Judge Dornbusch-Kim sits in **Pasadena**. She is widely regarded as one of the most respected appellate jurists in the federal system and has been mentioned in press accounts as a possible future Supreme Court nominee.

Her academic work focused on federal courts, civil procedure, and the law of equitable remedies. Her treatise *Equitable Remedies in Federal Civil Practice* (Oxford, 2010) is in its third edition.

She grew up in **Davis, California**, the daughter of two UC Davis biologists, and attended **Yale** as an undergraduate (Mathematics, 1982).

## Profile 10: Wilson J. Echebiri, JD '86

**Wilson Echebiri** is the founding partner of **Echebiri Mendez Reichmann LLP**, a Los Angeles plaintiff-side employment law firm widely regarded as one of the most successful in California in wage-and-hour class action litigation.

In 2023 the firm recovered an aggregate of approximately **$320 million** in class-wide settlements on behalf of its clients, who included workers in the warehouse-logistics, ride-share, and home-care industries.

Echebiri serves on the board of trustees of the **Berkeley Law Foundation** and endowed, in 2017, the **Echebiri Family Scholarship** for Berkeley Law students with demonstrated commitments to labor and employment law on the worker side.

He came to law school after two years organizing for the **United Farm Workers** in the Central Valley.

## Profile 11: Soraya Mukherjee-Glenn, JD '87

He came to law school after two years organizing for the **United Farm Workers** in the Central Valley.

## Profile 11: Soraya Mukherjee-Glenn, JD '87

**Soraya Mukherjee-Glenn** has spent the bulk of her career at the **California Public Utilities Commission**, where she has served as **Chief Administrative Law Judge** since 2019. She joined the CPUC as a staff ALJ in 1995 after seven years in private practice at **Morrison & Foerster** in San Francisco.

Mukherjee-Glenn has presided over many of the largest CPUC adjudicative proceedings of the past decade, including the post-2017 wildfire docket and the ongoing investigations into utility undergrounding plans. She has authored more than 240 published proposed decisions.

She is a graduate of **Pomona College** (1984) and holds an M.S. in Public Policy from the **Hertie School** in Berlin (1985, on a Fulbright).

## Profile 12: Marcus T. Holloway-Quigley, JD '87

**Marcus Holloway-Quigley** is the **Vice President for Worldwide Government Affairs** at **Lockheed Martin Corporation**, based in Bethesda, Maryland. He has held that position since 2019, after a long career that included six years at the **U.S. State Department** (1990-1996), seven years at the **U.S. House Foreign Affairs Committee** majority staff (1996-2003), and sixteen years at **Boeing Defense, Space & Security**.

Holloway-Quigley is a frequent speaker at Berkeley Law's national-security law programs. He has endowed an annual **Holloway-Quigley Lecture in National Security Practice**, the most recent of which was delivered in February 2024 by former Solicitor General Donald Verrilli.

## Profile 13: Yolanda Cesário-Vega, JD '88

**Yolanda Cesário-Vega** has spent her entire career — thirty-six years and counting — at the **East Bay Community Law Center**, the East Oakland legal services and clinical-education organization that Berkeley Law students helped found in the late 1980s.

Cesário-Vega started at the Center as a third-year Boalt student in the inaugural clinical cohort in 1987. She joined the staff after graduation, and has been the Center's **Executive Director** since 2002.

Under her leadership, the Center has grown into one of the largest legal-services organizations in the East Bay and continues to serve as the cornerstone of Berkeley Law's clinical curriculum, training generations of law students in the disciplines of housing law, family law, and economic-justice lawyering.

She received the **California Bar's Loren Miller Legal Services Award** in 2017.

## Profile 14: Khalil M. Foster-Iwasaki, JD '89

She received the **California Bar's Loren Miller Legal Services Award** in 2017.

## Profile 14: Khalil M. Foster-Iwasaki, JD '89

**Khalil Foster-Iwasaki** is the founder and CEO of **Cypress & Cedar Coffee Roasters**, a Bay Area specialty-coffee company with sixteen retail locations and a roasting facility in West Oakland. The company employs approximately 280 people and was, in 2023, named one of the *San Francisco Business Times*' Best Places to Work for the seventh consecutive year.

Foster-Iwasaki practiced law for only four years after Boalt — at the now-defunct firm of **Heller Ehrman White & McAuliffe** — before leaving the profession in 1993 to open his first coffee shop on College Avenue in Berkeley.

He has remained close to Berkeley Law as a regular speaker in the school's **Entrepreneurship and Innovation Practicum** and as a regular caterer (gratis) for the school's commencement events.

## A Note on Methodology

This feature was reported between June and September 2024 by *Transcript* staff, with research assistance from Berkeley Law's Alumni Relations office. Profiles were drafted from interviews conducted by the author with each subject, supplemented by review of public-record materials and prior published coverage.

The fourteen profiles above were selected by the editors from a longer list of more than ninety candidates suggested by the Alumni Relations office and by the *Transcript*'s editorial advisory board. The selection sought to balance professional, geographic, demographic, and graduation-year diversity. The selection is not intended as a ranking, and many distinguished classmates of the 1980s graduating classes have not been profiled here.

The editors invite readers to suggest alumni for future features at **transcript@law.berkeley.example**.

### Meeting 1 — September 13, 2024

**Location:** California Lawyers Association Conference Center, 400 Capitol Mall, Suite 650, Sacramento, CA, and via Zoom.

**Call to Order:** 10:03 a.m., Chair **Andrea L. Wei** presiding.

**Roll Call (Voting Members Present):**
- Andrea L. Wei, Chair
- Patrick O. Demming, Vice Chair
- Hanae Yoshida, Treasurer
- Roberta J. Kwan, Secretary
- Saul Castellanos
- Priya Ramaswamy
- Edward T. McNally
- Bettina S. Pollack-Brent
- Kim D. Onyebuchi
- Jeremy Holstrom
- Vincente A. Zaragoza

**Absent (Excused):** Lara Echeverria (sabbatical), Dorothea Walsh

**Non-Voting / Guests:**
- Carlos R. Acuña, CLA Sections Coordinator
- Henrietta Mintz, CLA staff liaison

#### 1. Approval of Minutes

Minutes of the June 21, 2024 meeting were approved as circulated. **Motion:** Demming. **Second:** Kwan. **Vote:** 11-0-0.

#### 2. Chair's Report

Chair Wei reported that Section membership stood at **1,847** paid members as of August 31, 2024, a 3.6% increase year-over-year. The Section continues to be the third-largest of the CLA's seventeen sections by membership and the second-largest by dues revenue.

Chair Wei attended the September 4 meeting of the **CLA Board of Trustees** in San Diego and reported back that the Board has approved the Section's request to maintain its existing dues structure ($95 active / $45 inactive / $25 new admittee) through FY 2026-2027.

#### 3. Treasurer's Report

Treasurer Yoshida reported the Section's operating account at **$324,118.42** as of August 31, 2024, against an FY 2024-2025 approved budget of **$298,500** in expenditures. The reserves account stood at $612,300, well above the Section's reserves policy floor of $250,000.

Notable line items:
- **Annual Meeting & Programs:** $84,210 budgeted; $11,407 expended YTD
- **Quarterly Bulletin (print + digital):** $46,000 budgeted; $19,002 expended YTD
- **Diversity Scholarship Program:** $24,000 budgeted; $4,500 expended YTD
- **Committee Travel:** $18,000 budgeted; $2,114 expended YTD

The Treasurer's report was received without objection.

#### 4. Standing Committee Reports

##### 4.a. Mediation Standards Committee

The Executive Committee is composed of fifteen voting members elected by the Section membership to staggered three-year terms. ## Northern California ADR Forum Confirms Featured Panel for 2024 Annual Meeting
### *"After the Bench: Former Federal Judges as Commercial Neutrals — Practice, Pitfalls, and Perception"*
### Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.) of Torres ADR Group LLC to Moderate Friday Morning Plenary Session at Palace Hotel

**SAN FRANCISCO, August 13, 2024** — The **Northern California ADR Forum** (NCADRF) today confirmed the lineup for one of the featured plenary panels of its 2024 Annual Meeting, to be held Friday, September 27, 2024, at the historic **Palace Hotel** in downtown San Francisco. The panel — *"After the Bench: Former Federal Judges as Commercial Neutrals — Practice, Pitfalls, and Perception"* — will convene from 10:15 a.m. to 11:45 a.m. PDT in the Palace Hotel's **Ralston Ballroom**.

The plenary will be moderated by **Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.)**, the founder of San Francisco-based **Torres ADR Group LLC** and a former United States District Judge for the Northern District of California. Joining Judge Torres on the panel are:

- **Hon. Linda M. Chao (Ret.)** — Neutral, JAMS;
- **Prof. Daniel Verma** — UC Hastings College of the Law; and
- **Marcia O'Donnell, Esq.** — General Counsel, Trellis Software, Inc.

### About the Moderator

Judge Torres served as a United States District Judge for the Northern District of California from 2004 until his retirement from the federal bench. He now maintains a full-time commercial arbitration and mediation practice in San Francisco. He is a **Fellow of the College of Commercial Arbitrators**, a member of the **American Arbitration Association's Commercial Panel**, and is admitted to practice in California, the District of Columbia, and New York.

Since opening Torres ADR Group LLC, Judge Torres has served as arbitrator, mediator, or special master in matters involving commercial contracts, technology licensing, financial services, and post-judgment disputes. He has been a regular speaker at programs of the American Bar Association's Dispute Resolution Section, the AAA-ICDR's Master Class series, and the Northern California ADR Forum.

### About the Panel

### About the Panel

The plenary will explore the career trajectory — increasingly common over the past two decades — from federal judicial service into private commercial dispute resolution. Panelists will address how former judges adapt their decision-making style to the arbitral context, manage party expectations, and navigate the market dynamics of an ADR market crowded with retired-judge neutrals.

### About the Northern California ADR Forum

The Northern California ADR Forum is a membership organization of approximately 940 California ADR professionals — arbitrators, mediators, special masters, advocates, and academics — based primarily in the Bay Area, Sacramento, and the Central Coast. The Forum holds an annual meeting in San Francisco each fall, publishes a quarterly bulletin (the *Forum Review*), and operates programming and mentorship initiatives focused on emerging neutrals and on diversity in the field.

The Forum was founded in 1996 as the **Bay Area Mediator and Arbitrator Society** and adopted its current name in 2008. Its current president is **Hannah Reilly-Petrov**, General Counsel of Solstice Devices, Inc.

### About Torres ADR Group LLC

**Torres ADR Group LLC** is a boutique full-service neutral practice founded by Judge Torres following his retirement from the federal bench. The practice is based in San Francisco's Financial District and serves clients throughout the United States and internationally. Areas of practice include commercial arbitration and mediation, post-judgment disputes, technology and intellectual-property matters, and complex multi-party disputes.

### About Featured Co-Panelist Hon. Linda M. Chao (Ret.)

### About Featured Co-Panelist Hon. Linda M. Chao (Ret.)

**Hon. Linda M. Chao (Ret.)**, a JAMS neutral based in Los Angeles, served as a United States District Judge for the Central District of California from 2007 to 2022. Judge Chao is a Fellow of the College of Commercial Arbitrators and serves on the AAA Commercial Panel and the JAMS National Arbitration Panel. She has been recognized in *Chambers USA* as a leading ADR neutral in California since 2023.

### About Featured Co-Panelist Prof. Daniel Verma

### About Featured Co-Panelist Prof. Daniel Verma

**Prof. Daniel Verma** is the Hochberg Professor of Law at UC Hastings College of the Law (now UC College of the Law, San Francisco), where he teaches courses in civil procedure, federal courts, and the law of arbitration. He is the author of the leading hornbook *The Law and Practice of Commercial Arbitration in the United States* (Foundation Press, 4th ed. 2024) and a frequent expert witness in vacatur proceedings.

### About Featured Co-Panelist Marcia O'Donnell, Esq.

### About Featured Co-Panelist Marcia O'Donnell, Esq.

**Marcia O'Donnell, Esq.** is the General Counsel of **Trellis Software, Inc.**, a privately-held enterprise software company headquartered in Mountain View, California. She joined Trellis in 2018 after twelve years in private practice at **Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati**, where she specialized in commercial disputes and corporate governance matters. Ms. O'Donnell is a 2006 graduate of Yale Law School and a 2003 graduate of Stanford University.

### Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.) — Moderator

Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.) is the founder of **Torres ADR Group LLC**, a San Francisco-based commercial-arbitration and mediation practice. Judge Torres served as a United States District Judge for the Northern District of California from 2004 until his retirement from the federal bench. Prior to his judicial appointment, he was a partner at a major San Francisco law firm, where he led the firm's complex commercial litigation practice for over a decade. He clerked, early in his career, for a Justice of the United States Supreme Court.

Judge Torres is a Fellow of the College of Commercial Arbitrators, a member of the American Arbitration Association's Commercial Panel and Large Complex Case Panel, and is admitted to practice in California, the District of Columbia, and New York. He has served as arbitrator, mediator, or special master in commercial, financial, technology, intellectual-property, and complex multi-party disputes throughout the United States and internationally.

Judge Torres is a regular speaker at programs of the American Bar Association's Dispute Resolution Section, the AAA-ICDR's Master Class series, the Bar Association of San Francisco, and the Northern California ADR Forum. He has authored several scholarly articles on commercial arbitration practice, including a 2022 article in the *Northwestern Journal of Dispute Resolution* on the role of former-judge neutrals in post-Viking River PAGA matters.

He received his undergraduate degree from a prominent East Coast university and his JD from a leading West Coast law school. He lives in San Francisco with his family.

## Supplementary Attachment D — Forum History and Mission

The Northern California ADR Forum was founded in 1996 as the **Bay Area Mediator and Arbitrator Society** by a small group of practitioners — including the late **John Tao**, **Patricia Anders-Falk**, and **Robert Mendelson-Owen** — who sought to create a professional community for the then-emerging cohort of full-time neutrals in the Bay Area.

The Society incorporated as a California nonprofit in 1997 and adopted its current name, the **Northern California ADR Forum**, in 2008 in recognition of its expanded geographic and professional scope.

The Forum's mission, as stated in its current bylaws, is:

The Forum's mission, as stated in its current bylaws, is:

> "To advance the practice of alternative dispute resolution in Northern California through programming, scholarship, mentorship, and advocacy; to promote diversity, equity, and inclusion in the field; and to support the continued professional development of ADR practitioners across all stages of career."

The Forum currently has approximately 940 active members. Membership is open to all California-based ADR practitioners, including arbitrators, mediators, special masters, advocates, and academics. Annual dues are $185 for full members, $95 for academic members, and $45 for student members.

The Forum's principal annual events are:

The Forum's principal annual events are:

- **The Annual Meeting** (late September, San Francisco) — the Forum's flagship two-day event, featuring plenary panels, breakouts, awards, and networking.
- **The Winter Workshop** (January, location varies) — a one-day intensive on a focused topic.
- **The Spring Symposium** (April, often paired with an academic institution) — a half-day scholarly program.
- **The Summer Reception** (June, San Francisco) — an informal members-only social event.

The Forum also publishes a quarterly bulletin, the *Forum Review*, with original scholarship, practice notes, and event coverage.

# "You Stop Wearing the Robe, You Don't Stop Thinking Like a Judge"

### An Interview with Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.)
*Conducted by Rachel Imanishi for the* California Dispute Resolution Quarterly *— Vol. 18, No. 2 (Spring 2024)*

---

**CDRQ:** Judge Torres, thank you for sitting down with us. You've now been off the bench for about four years. Does it still feel like a transition, or has the new chapter become the normal one?

**TORRES:** It's the normal one. I'll tell you, the first six months I kept reaching for the gavel out of habit, and there is no gavel in a hotel conference room in Embarcadero. But after thirty-plus years on the bench, you internalize a certain rhythm — read the papers, frame the issues, get to the point — and that rhythm translates pretty cleanly into arbitration. The cases are different, but the discipline is the same.

**CDRQ:** Many former judges say the transition is harder than they expected.

**CDRQ:** Many former judges say the transition is harder than they expected.

**TORRES:** I think the surprise for me wasn't the work — it was the marketing. Sitting judges don't have business cards. I had to learn how to talk to general counsel as a service provider, not as a tribunal. That took some adjustment. I lean on my associates at the Group for the administrative side. I'd rather be reading a 60-page brief than sending invoices, and at $1,450 an hour I'd like to think clients prefer it that way too.

**CDRQ:** Looking back at your bench years, what cases shaped you most?

**CDRQ:** Looking back at your bench years, what cases shaped you most?

**TORRES:** Two stand out. The *Halverson* antitrust matter — the indirect-purchaser certification — taught me a lot about how to manage a record that has more economists than fact witnesses. And on the employment side, *Reyes v. Aldritch* was instructive because it forced me to confront how mechanically courts sometimes apply the FLSA collective-action standard. Both of those have come up obliquely in arbitrations I've handled since.

**CDRQ:** Do you see meaningful differences between mediating and arbitrating?

**CDRQ:** Do you see meaningful differences between mediating and arbitrating?

**TORRES:** They use different muscles. As an arbitrator, I'm decisional — I'm going to write an award, and the parties want my judgment, not my therapy. As a mediator I'm trying to find the deal that everyone can hate equally. I prefer arbitration, frankly, because I'm a decision-maker by training. But I take both, and a lot of my mediations these days are med-arb hybrids.

**CDRQ:** What's the most common mistake you see from counsel in front of you?

**TORRES:** Overbriefing. People file 50 pages where 15 would do. I tell counsel up front: I read everything, but I weight the first ten pages most. If you've buried the lede, you've already lost ground.

**CDRQ:** You've also been outspoken about confidentiality norms in commercial arbitration.

**TORRES:** I think the profession needs to grow up about it. Confidentiality protects the parties, not the process. We can publish redacted awards. We can talk about doctrine. The mystique hurts us more than it helps.

**CDRQ:** Any plans to slow down?

**TORRES:** I'll be 69 in November. My calendar is full through next spring. I told my wife I'd cut back at seventy, but I've been saying that for a while.

**CDRQ:** Thank you for your time, Judge.

**TORRES:** My pleasure.

# Retired N.D. Cal. Judge Tapped to Mediate Sprawling Ad-Tech Antitrust Dispute

**The Recorder** — May 2, 2024
*By Eliza Chun, Staff Reporter*

Former U.S. District Judge Michael A. Torres has been selected as the neutral mediator in a multidistrict ad-tech antitrust dispute pitting a coalition of regional newspaper publishers against a consortium of digital advertising exchanges, sources familiar with the matter confirmed this week.

The selection of Torres, 68, signals a shift toward seasoned federal judges in complex commercial mediation, where parties increasingly seek neutrals with deep antitrust pedigree. Torres, who stepped down from the bench in early 2020 after fifteen years as a district judge in the Northern District of California, was nominated to the bench in 2004 and has, since his retirement, built one of the busier ADR practices on the West Coast.

"Judge Torres is famously impatient with theatrics," said one San Francisco litigator who has appeared before him in three private arbitrations. "He reads everything. He shows up having already framed the issues. You don't waste his time."

That reputation was forged during a decade and a half on the federal bench, where Torres developed a particular expertise in antitrust class certification — including the closely-watched *In re Halverson Semiconductor* litigation, in which he certified an indirect-purchaser class in 2019. The opinion has since been cited more than 80 times by federal district courts.

Before his judicial appointment, Torres spent two decades as a litigation partner at Coleman, Pryce & Whitfield in San Francisco, where he tried more than thirty cases to verdict. He clerked for Judge Patricia M. Vallejo of the Ninth Circuit immediately after graduating from Berkeley Law in the early 1980s.

The ad-tech mediation, which involves more than $400 million in alleged damages spread across seven coordinated actions, is expected to span four to six months. A spokesperson for Torres ADR Group declined to confirm the engagement, citing the confidentiality of private dispute resolution.

Torres opened his San Francisco-based ADR shop in mid-2020, shortly after concluding his service on the bench. The firm now lists six neutrals on its roster but Torres himself handles the bulk of high-stakes commercial matters. According to publicly available reports, his calendar typically books out nine to twelve months in advance.

He is also a Fellow of the College of Commercial Arbitrators, which he joined in 2022, and serves on the AAA's Commercial Panel. Earlier this year, he was a keynote speaker at the Northern California ADR Forum, where he urged practitioners to "stop pretending arbitration is litigation with a smaller jury box."

Sources close to the ad-tech mediation said an initial joint session is expected to convene in early June at neutral facilities in downtown San Francisco. Whether the parties reach resolution before trial — a related action is set for trial in the District of Delaware in October — may turn in significant part on Torres's well-honed ability to push sophisticated litigants toward unsentimental compromise.

## I. Procedural Background

**Before:** Hon. Michael A. Torres (Ret.), Sole Arbitrator

**Issued:** August 14, 2023, San Francisco, California

---

## I. Procedural Background

This matter arises out of a Master Services Agreement dated March 11, 2019 (the "MSA"), under which Claimant Meridian Analytics, Inc. ("Meridian") agreed to provide custom data-engineering and predictive-modeling services to Respondent Karsten Biosciences Corporation ("Karsten") in connection with Karsten's Phase II clinical trial pipeline. Section 18.4 of the MSA contains a broad arbitration clause designating the American Arbitration Association and selecting California substantive law.

Meridian filed its Demand for Arbitration on October 7, 2022, asserting claims for breach of contract, breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, and quantum meruit, and seeking damages in the amount of $4.62 million plus prejudgment interest. Karsten filed an Answer and Counterclaim on November 18, 2022, asserting breach of warranty and seeking offset of approximately $1.8M.

The Arbitrator was appointed by AAA on December 9, 2022, following the parties' inability to agree on a neutral from the AAA Commercial Roster strike list. A preliminary hearing was conducted on January 11, 2023. A merits hearing was held over five days, from June 5 through June 9, 2023, at the AAA hearing facility in San Francisco. The parties submitted post-hearing briefs on July 14, 2023.

## II. Findings of Fact

After considering the testimony of nine fact witnesses and two expert witnesses, and reviewing the documentary record (Exhibits C-1 through C-214 and R-1 through R-156), the Arbitrator finds as follows:

1. The MSA's Statement of Work No. 3 ("SOW-3"), executed July 22, 2020, required Meridian to deliver a validated predictive model with a minimum area-under-curve ("AUC") of 0.82 on Karsten's held-out validation dataset.
2. Meridian delivered an initial model on January 4, 2021, achieving an AUC of 0.79. After two iteration cycles, the model achieved AUC of 0.84 on November 9, 2021, satisfying the SOW-3 acceptance criterion.
3. Karsten's refusal to issue the acceptance certificate required under Section 7.2 of the MSA was not made in good faith. *See Carma Developers (Cal.), Inc. v. Marathon Dev. Cal., Inc.*, 2 Cal. 4th 342, 372 (1992).

## III. Conclusions of Law

## III. Conclusions of Law

Under California law, a party may not invoke a discretionary acceptance provision in a manner that frustrates the other party's reasonable expectations under the contract. *Guz v. Bechtel Nat'l, Inc.*, 24 Cal. 4th 317, 349–50 (2000); *Locke v. Warner Bros., Inc.*, 57 Cal. App. 4th 354, 363 (1997). The Arbitrator concludes that Karsten breached Sections 7.2 and 12.1 of the MSA.

## IV. Award

For the foregoing reasons, the Arbitrator AWARDS Claimant the principal sum of **$3,847,206.00**, together with prejudgment interest at the California statutory rate of 10% per annum from December 1, 2021 through the date of this Award. Karsten's counterclaims are DENIED in their entirety.

Administrative fees and expenses of the AAA totaling $74,300 and the compensation of the Arbitrator totaling $182,750 shall be borne 75% by Respondent and 25% by Claimant.

This Award resolves all claims and counterclaims submitted to this arbitration.

So ordered this 14th day of August, 2023.

/s/ *Hon. Michael A. Torres (Ret.)*
Sole Arbitrator

# Northern California ADR Roundtable Tackles Disclosure, Diversity, and the Boom in Mass Arbitration

*The San Francisco Daily Journal — Friday, October 25, 2024 — Section B, Front Page*
By Nathaniel Park-Hwang, Senior Reporter

---

## At the City Club, a Conversation about the Future

SAN FRANCISCO — On the seventh floor of the **City Club of San Francisco** on Wednesday evening, a panel of nine attorneys, four sitting judges, six retired neutrals, and the general counsel of three California companies gathered around a configuration of tables shaped to keep almost every speaker visible to almost every listener. The result was an animated, occasionally contentious, three-hour conversation about the state of alternative dispute resolution in Northern California — and where it is heading.

The event was the **2024 Northern California ADR Roundtable**, an annual gathering convened by the **Bar Association of San Francisco** in cooperation with the **Northern California ADR Forum**, the **California Lawyers Association ADR Section**, and the *Daily Journal* itself. The roundtable, now in its eleventh year, is held under a modified Chatham House Rule: speakers may be identified, but specific attribution of remarks is permitted only with the speaker's consent.

Some forty-five additional members of the bar and judiciary attended as observers, including this reporter.

## A Crowded Agenda

The agenda, distributed in printed form at the door, listed six discussion topics:

By the end of the evening, items one through four had received considerable air time. Items five and six were briefly addressed in the final twenty minutes.

## Disclosure After Honeycutt

The roundtable opened with a thirty-minute conversation on the implications of *Honeycutt v. Vertix Solutions*, the June 2024 Court of Appeal decision that vacated a $42 million arbitration award on the grounds of the chair's failure to disclose a prior mediation involving a parent entity of the prevailing respondent.

**Hon. Cassandra L. Lin (Ret.)**, a JAMS neutral and former presiding justice of the Court of Appeal's Sixth District, called *Honeycutt* a "wake-up call disguised as an outlier." Justice Lin argued that the court's reasoning, while framed narrowly, signaled an underlying judicial impatience with neutrals who treat the California disclosure standards as a checklist rather than a continuing inquiry.

"Disclosure is a culture," she said. "If you only think about it the day the case opens, you are doing it wrong. You should be thinking about it every time a new exhibit list comes in, every time you read a witness name you didn't expect."

**Daniela Marchetti-Ruiz**, a partner at **Quinn Emanuel** who has represented parties opposing major arbitration awards, agreed but pressed further. "The standard is now, in effect, that 'I didn't realize the connection' is no longer a defense. The question is what an experienced neutral *should* have realized."

Counsel on the other side — **Adrián O. Vega**, a partner at **Kirkland & Ellis** who frequently represents prevailing arbitration parties in vacatur litigation — pushed back. "We are going to manufacture a vacatur industry," he warned. "Every losing party will now spend three months data-mining the chair's prior cases for an undisclosed connection. The cost of awards will go up. The finality of awards will go down. That is not what the FAA was designed to deliver."

The conversation continued for the better part of forty minutes before the moderator, **Prof. Marisol Echeverria-Kim** of UC Hastings, moved the group to the next agenda item.

## Mass Arbitration and the Cost Question

The mass-arbitration discussion was framed around the increasingly common practice of plaintiff-side firms filing tens of thousands of individual consumer arbitration demands against a single corporate respondent simultaneously, often with the effect of triggering very large administrative-fee obligations under the respondent's own arbitration agreement.

**Hannah Reilly-Petrov**, general counsel of the consumer-electronics company **Solstice Devices, Inc.** (which has faced a 14,000-claim mass-arbitration filing in the past 18 months) called the trend "a perfectly logical response to a regime we, the corporate defense bar, designed." She drew a small laugh from the room.

"We wanted arbitration. We got arbitration. We got it 14,000 times. Now we have to figure out how to make it work."

"We wanted arbitration. We got arbitration. We got it 14,000 times. Now we have to figure out how to make it work."

**Eleanor Goss-Adisa**, a partner at **Gibbs Law Group** who has represented plaintiffs in three of California's largest mass-arbitration matters, was unsentimental. "The fee schedules are what the companies wrote. If they want to renegotiate, the time to do that is in good faith with class counsel, not in a motion to compel arbitration designed to escape class treatment."

The panel discussed at length the AAA's and JAMS's experimentations with **mass-arbitration protocols**, including bellwether-track procedures and modified fee structures. **Carlos Reyes-Lim**, the AAA's regional vice president for the Pacific Coast, sketched the AAA's most recent (September 2024) protocol revisions and took questions from the room for nearly twenty minutes.

## The Panels Boom

The third topic — the rapid expansion of the major neutral-services providers' panels — produced perhaps the evening's most candid exchange.

The third topic — the rapid expansion of the major neutral-services providers' panels — produced perhaps the evening's most candid exchange.

**Hon. Rosalind Quirk (Ret.)**, who retired from the Alameda County Superior Court in 2018 and now mediates and arbitrates with **Judicate West**, observed that the practical effect of the panel expansion has been a flooding of the market with retired judges, many of whom market themselves identically and few of whom have meaningfully differentiated practices.

"There are now over three hundred and fifty retired California judges holding themselves out as full-time neutrals," Quirk said. "I doubt the market needs that many. The next five years will be a reckoning."

She added: "The retired-judge brand still carries weight. But it carries less weight than it did a decade ago. Parties are starting to ask what you actually *do* with disputes, not just what your title used to be."

Several members of the panel agreed. **Marcia Quan**, general counsel of **Bayhill Bioworks**, said her company's selection process had become considerably more granular over the past five years: "We used to pick a former judge with a good Yelp page. We now do back-channel calls, we review past awards if we can find them, and we ask about hearing room demeanor specifically. The market matured because we, the buyers, matured."

**Prof. Echeverria-Kim** asked the panel to address the specific question of how the expansion had affected diversity on the panels.

**Prof. Echeverria-Kim** asked the panel to address the specific question of how the expansion had affected diversity on the panels.

The conversation that followed lasted nearly forty-five minutes.

## Diversity on Neutral Panels

By common consensus in the room, the major providers have made measurable progress on demographic diversity at the entry tier of the panels but considerably less progress on what one speaker called the "rotation tier" — the subset of neutrals who actually receive the majority of party selections in commercial matters.

**Karima Diop-Engelhart**, who chairs the **National Association of Women Lawyers**' arbitration subcommittee, offered statistics: of approximately 1,400 neutrals on the major California-active commercial panels, women constitute approximately 31% of total listings but only 18% of selected appointments in matters with claims over $5 million. Underrepresented minority neutrals constitute approximately 17% of listings and 9% of selected appointments at the same claim-size threshold.

"The roster is one thing," Diop-Engelhart said. "The pipeline of who actually gets picked is another. We have lots of names on the roster. We have very few on the working panel."

Several proposals were canvassed during the discussion, including JAMS's pilot **"diverse striker rule"** (under which the AAA and JAMS would, in agreed circumstances, expand party-strike lists to include additional diverse candidates), corporate-side commitments to include at least one diverse candidate on every strike list, and the use of **disclosure-of-prior-selections** statistics by the major providers.

Vega from Kirkland was skeptical of the most aggressive proposals: "If I am defending a $40 million dispute, my obligation is to my client. I have to pick the neutral I think gives my client the best chance. I support a wider roster. I oppose anything that constrains the strike list."

Goss-Adisa pushed back: "What you call 'best chance' is, in practice, 'most familiar.' That is the problem we are trying to solve."

The exchange continued without easy resolution.

## A Word on Absences and Panelist Logistics

The exchange continued without easy resolution.

## A Word on Absences and Panelist Logistics

Echeverria-Kim, the moderator, noted near the end of the evening that the convening had been timed to coincide with the **Northern California ADR Forum's** broader fall programming and to involve as many of the region's senior neutrals as possible. Several anticipated panelists, she noted, had been unable to attend due to scheduling conflicts: among them **Hon. Patricia M. Reyes (Ret.)**, **Hon. Brian Hutch (Ret.)**, and **Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.)** of Torres ADR Group LLC, all of whom had been listed in earlier drafts of the program. The roundtable continued as planned in their absence.

## AI in Drafting and Document Review

## AI in Drafting and Document Review

In the final twenty minutes, **Prof. Echeverria-Kim** turned to the question of AI's impact on the practice. The discussion was brief but pointed.

**Justice Lin** said she had begun, very cautiously, using a commercial LLM-based tool for first-pass summary of multi-thousand-page transcript excerpts in larger matters, with careful human verification. She described the tool as "a junior associate who never sleeps and occasionally tells you something that isn't true."

**Quan** of Bayhill said her company had updated its outside-counsel guidelines in 2024 to require disclosure of any use of generative AI in drafting filings, including arbitration briefs. The provision, she said, had been largely uncontroversial in negotiation.

**Vega** worried that AI tools, if used by the neutral in award drafting, raise unsettled disclosure and waiver questions. "I do not know what I would do if I learned a panel had used an LLM to draft my client's award," he said. "I am not sure my motion to vacate writes itself. I am not sure it doesn't."

The conversation was cut short by the closing-time announcement from the City Club staff.

## Mediation Confidentiality, Briefly

In the closing minutes, **Hon. Sanjay Singh-Allard (Ret.)**, a former Contra Costa County Superior Court judge now affiliated with **ADR Services, Inc.**, briefly addressed the State Bar's 2023 study commission report on **mediation confidentiality**.

"The legislature has shown no appetite for the *Cassel v. Superior Court* fix the commission recommended," Singh-Allard observed. "Practitioners should plan accordingly. If you want a malpractice claim against your mediation counsel preserved, you may need to negotiate it expressly. The default rule has not moved."

## Coda

The roundtable ended a few minutes after 9:00 p.m. Many participants moved to an informal post-event reception in the City Club library, where, on a tray near the bar, a small printed card listed each of the evening's discussion topics and the suggested follow-up readings for each.

The 2025 Roundtable is scheduled for late October. Registration is expected to open in mid-summer.

The Northern California ADR Roundtable began in 2014 as a small invitation-only gathering convened by then–BASF President **Tomas Lim-Cardoza** in collaboration with the Northern California ADR Forum. The first Roundtable was held at the Olympic Club and drew approximately 22 participants.

By 2018 the Roundtable had grown to roughly 50 participants and had moved to the larger ballroom at the City Club. By 2022, with the addition of a third co-sponsor (the CLA ADR Section), the Roundtable had grown to roughly 75 participants and a more formal panel format.

## Sidebar: A Reporter's Notebook — Three Observations from the Floor

Throughout the evening I made notes that did not fit cleanly into the main reporting. Three of those notes are reproduced below.

### Observation 1: The Generational Shift Is Real

Of the approximately seventy-five attorneys and judges in the room, I counted roughly eighteen under the age of forty. That is more than I have seen at any prior Roundtable. The generational shift in the California ADR community — from a profession dominated by retired-judge neutrals trained in the 1970s and 1980s to a profession increasingly populated by younger practitioners — is becoming visible.

The younger attendees, in conversation at the post-event reception, were notably more interested in AI tools, mass-arbitration economics, and the structural questions raised by the diversity discussion than in disclosure doctrine or the case-law of vacatur. This is unsurprising. But the generational gap between what the senior practitioners want to discuss and what the junior practitioners want to discuss is now wide enough that a future Roundtable might productively break into two simultaneous tracks.

### Observation 2: The Corporate Counsel Are Driving the Market

### Observation 2: The Corporate Counsel Are Driving the Market

I was struck by how forcefully Marcia Quan of Bayhill Bioworks and Hannah Reilly-Petrov of Solstice Devices spoke during the panel diversity and panel-expansion discussions. The corporate counsel in the room were not a passive consumer base. They were articulating very specific demands: better metrics, more transparent disclosure of selection statistics, more accountability for the major providers.

This is, I think, the under-reported story of the past three years in California ADR. The supply side of the neutral market has been the focus of trade-press coverage. The demand side — corporate America, increasingly sophisticated and increasingly willing to use its leverage — is reshaping the market more aggressively than the supply side knows.

### Observation 3: The Absent Speakers Got Discussed Anyway

Several of the practitioners on the published-but-not-attended list — Hon. Patricia M. Reyes (Ret.), Hon. Brian Hutch (Ret.), and Hon. Michael Torres (Ret.) of Torres ADR Group LLC — were mentioned only once in the official discussion, in the moderator's housekeeping notes early in the evening. None of their absences became substantive topics.

This is, I think, the right outcome. The Roundtable is not about which neutrals are most prominent; it is about the structural questions facing the profession. The convening organizations should be commended for keeping the discussion at that altitude.
